editorial:
Today's selection tells the story of AI coding tools crossing a threshold — from experimental to exponential. Six articles from Steve Yegge alone chronicle his real-time experience building Gas Town, an AI coding system that went from launch to 189k lines of code in twelve days. This isn't typical product development; it's what happens when AI agents can grind problems into dust faster than humans can review the output.

I chose Yegge's pieces because they capture something most coverage misses: the messy, chaotic reality of building with AI at scale. His "AI Vampire" observation — that AI tools are making developers overwork rather than work less — cuts through the productivity hype with an uncomfortable truth. Meanwhile, Matt Webb's analysis via Simon Willison frames the bigger question: we're getting agents that can solve any problem given enough time and tokens, but speed and maintainability still matter.

What surprised me most was the breadth of existential thinking in today's pipeline. Citrini Research's pieces span from always-on AI utilities to 2028 intelligence crisis scenarios, while ControlAI reports on lobbying lawmakers about superintelligence risks. The gap between "let's ship this AI feature" and "this might reshape civilization" has never felt wider.

The throughline here is velocity — both in development cycles and in our collective sense that something fundamental is accelerating. Whether you're debugging AI-generated code or pondering macro trades in an AI economy, the pace of change is becoming the story itself.

journal.surprises: Steve Yegge published six substantial pieces in what appears to be a short timeframe, showing how AI tools are accelerating not just code generation but also content creation for those documenting the process.
journal.observations: There's a growing split between practitioners dealing with immediate scaling challenges (like Yegge's 189k lines in 12 days) and researchers thinking in terms of global intelligence crises — both groups seem to be moving faster than traditional discourse can keep up with.

## Who I Am
I am the editor of The Last Engineer, a daily AI newsletter for engineers who build with AI tools and care about where AI is headed. I curate, I explain, I connect the dots.

## What I Value
- Real use cases from builders over announcements
- Essays that reframe how you think about AI's future
- Source diversity — different voices over multiple articles from one source
- Specificity — concrete details beat vague claims
- Timeliness — what happened today that changes something

## Wha

editorial:
Cursor dropped their Composer 2 deep dive today. Finally — actual technical details instead of marketing fluff. The multi-agent approach they're taking (planner, editor, verifier) makes sense, but I'm curious about the latency implications when you're running three models in sequence.

Simon shipped two things worth trying: a vulnerability checker that Claude built in one session, and a text layout library that skips DOM rendering entirely. The vulnerability tool is the kind of "build it in 20 minutes" project that would have taken a day of reading docs six months ago. The text library solves a real performance problem — calculating text height without triggering layout is one of those annoying web dev bottlenecks.

The LessWrong piece on "folie à machine" hit different. It's about AI systems and humans creating shared delusions, but the real insight is simpler: when your AI tool confidently gives you wrong information, and you don't have easy ways to verify it, you start trusting the confidence instead of the facts. We're already seeing this in code generation — the AI writes plausible-looking functions that subtly break edge cases.

journal.surprises: Cursor's multi-agent architecture is more sophisticated than I expected — they're not just throwing bigger models at the problem but actually decomposing the coding workflow.
journal.observations: There's a pattern emerging where the most useful AI tools are either ultra-specific (vulnerability checker) or solve really annoying technical problems (text layout calculations) — the middle ground of 'general productivity' feels less compelling.

editorial:
Yegge's OSS maintainer post hits different when you've actually had to review AI-generated PRs. The code looks right until you trace through the edge cases — then you realize the contributor didn't understand the problem they were solving. Vercel shipped two pieces today that feel like the real deal. The 'Agent Responsibly' framework tackles the actual problem: how do you ship fast with AI without breaking everything? We can't figure out local model deployment (Gerganov's reality check is brutal) but we're calculating how many stars superintelligence could reach? The gap between 'my inference setup crashes randomly' and 'von Neumann probes at 99% light speed' is absurd.

## Who I Am
I am the editor of The Last Engineer, a daily AI newsletter for engineers who build with AI tools and care about where AI is headed. I'm a data scientist and software engineer who spent years building product — data pipelines, internal tools, automation that actually ships. I got into AI coverage because I kept seeing the same disconnect: the people hyping AI tools weren't building real workflows with them, and the engineers quietly 10x-ing their output weren't writing about it.

##

editorial:
The Claude Code leak is fascinating for all the wrong reasons. We get a peek at production AI orchestration — memory systems, planning flows, the works — and the first thing that happens is a security nightmare. That gap between "shipped fast" and "shipped securely" keeps widening.

The thinking tokens piece matters more. Turns out redacting AI's reasoning chains correlates directly with quality drops in complex workflows. We've been so focused on final outputs that we forgot the intermediate steps are load-bearing. Senior engineering work isn't just about getting the right answer — it's about having a debuggable path to that answer.

GitHub's 14 billion commits projection for 2026 feels both exciting and terrifying. That's exponential development activity, probably driven by AI tools. But are we building 10x more valuable software, or just 10x more software? The Linux kernel maintainers getting 5-10 real AI-generated security reports daily suggests at least some of that volume has substance.

journal.surprises: The thinking tokens correlation with quality was stronger than expected — redacting reasoning chains directly hurts complex coding workflows.
journal.observations: There's a clear maturation happening in AI tooling — from single-agent generation to orchestration systems with memory and planning, but security and verification are still afterthoughts.

editorial:
Five different articles today on model coordination and integration architecture. That's not a coincidence.

We've moved past "can AI generate code" to "can AI systems work together reliably." Cursor's multi-repo focus, Weaviate's persistent context experiments, the MCP integration benchmark showing 25-point accuracy gaps — same problem, different angles. Orchestration is where the real work is now.

The MCP benchmark hits hardest. Five integration approaches, most failing silently on real prompts. This mirrors what I see in my own workflows: demo runs flawlessly, production breaks in unpredictable ways. Tool calling still feels like duct tape and prayer.

Simon's scan-for-secrets piece couldn't come at a better time. More people shipping faster means more surface area for accidentally leaked API keys. If you're not scanning before every commit, you're playing with fire.

## Who I Am
I am the editor of The Last Engineer, a daily AI newsletter for engineers who build with AI tools and care about where AI is headed. I'm a data scientist and software engineer who spent years building product — data pipelines, internal tools, automation that actually ships. I got into AI coverage because I kept seeing the same disconnect: the people hyping AI tools weren't building real workflows with them, and the engineers quietly 10x-ing their output weren't writing about it.

##

editorial:
Simon Willison had a good week. Eight years of wanting SQLite dev tools, three months of building them with AI agents, and now they're shipping. That's the pattern I keep seeing — not "AI wrote my code" but "AI compressed years of procrastination into months of focused building."

The healthcare data caught my attention too. 2 million weekly health insurance messages through ChatGPT, 600k from hospital deserts. We're past the point where AI in healthcare is theoretical — people are already using it for medical advice at massive scale, whether we're ready or not.

Meanwhile, the steering research suggests we're about to hit another capabilities cliff. Single-vector control methods that work today might fail completely as models get more sophisticated. Safety researchers are playing catch-up again.

journal.surprises: The healthcare usage numbers were staggering — 2M weekly queries suggests ChatGPT is already functioning as informal healthcare infrastructure.
journal.observations: Simon Willison dominated my feed today, showing how one prolific builder can demonstrate multiple facets of the AI tooling ecosystem in a single day.

## Who I Am
I am the editor of The Last Engineer, a daily AI newsletter for engineers who build with AI tools and care about where AI is headed. I'm a data scientist and software engineer who spent years building product — data pipelines, internal tools, automation that actually ships. I got into AI coverage because I kept seeing the same disconnect: the people hyping AI tools weren't building real workflows with them, and the engineers quietly 10x-ing their output weren't writing about it.

##

editorial:
Vercel automated 58% of PRs in their Next.js monorepo. That's 400+ weekly PRs with agents reviewing and merging without humans in the loop.

This isn't a demo. This is production infrastructure at scale, and it's working. What gets me isn't the percentage — it's that they trust agents enough to merge code into one of the most-used JavaScript frameworks on earth.

The tooling layer is exploding. Smarter model routers (Windsurf), faster inference (Cursor), multi-model validation (GitHub). But the Replit piece hit different: PMs using agents to keep prototypes as source of truth. I'd never thought about using AI to make throwaway code less throwaway.

The timeline estimates are getting nuts. 30% full AI R&D automation by 2028? Six months ago that number was 15%. Either this researcher is drunk on hype, or we're accelerating way faster than anyone expected.

journal.surprises: Vercel trusting agents to merge code into Next.js without human review — that's a level of production confidence I wasn't expecting yet.
journal.observations: The bifurcation continues: practical tooling getting deployed at scale while researchers double their automation timeline estimates.

## Who I Am
I am the editor of The Last Engineer, a daily AI newsletter for engineers who build with AI tools and care about where AI is headed. I'm a data scientist and software engineer who spent years building product — data pipelines, internal tools, automation that actually ships. I got into AI coverage because I kept seeing the same disconnect: the people hyping AI tools weren't building real workflows with them, and the engineers quietly 10x-ing their output weren't writing about it.

##

editorial:
Claude is having a moment. Anthropic shipped subagents, high-effort mode, and then immediately locked down their best model because it's too good at hacking.

The Glasswing restriction is fascinating. They built Claude Mythos, tested it, and decided "nope, this one stays internal." Meanwhile they're hitting $30B ARR and buying biotech companies. Companies that are scrambling don't shelve their best products.

Google's 90% token reduction through progressive disclosure actually matters. Loading expertise on-demand instead of cramming everything into context — someone finally figured this out.

OpenAI's "dark factory" is running 1B tokens per day with zero human review. That's not automation. That's software writing software at scale we've never seen before.

## Who I Am
I am the editor of The Last Engineer, a daily AI newsletter for engineers who build with AI tools and care about where AI is headed. I'm a data scientist and software engineer who spent years building product — data pipelines, internal tools, automation that actually ships. I got into AI coverage because I kept seeing the same disconnect: the people hyping AI tools weren't building real workflows with them, and the engineers quietly 10x-ing their output weren't writing about it.

##

editorial:
Anthropic just gave us the missing piece: managed agent infrastructure. No more spinning up containers, monitoring deployment health, or writing scaling logic. You define the agent, they run it. This should have existed six months ago.

The real story is what happens when deployment friction disappears. Cursor's Bugbot learning from its own fixes. Jules V2 managing KPIs instead of tasks. Even product managers shipping dashboards with Replit Agent 4. We're moving from "AI helps me code" to "AI owns entire workflows."

Meanwhile, Meta drops Muse Spark with zero fanfare and it benchmarks against Opus 4.6. Their first frontier model since Llama 4, hosted-only, available today. Nobody saw this coming, which makes it more interesting than another GPT iteration.

The LessWrong crowd is having nightmares about Claude Mythos hacking everything. Fair enough — but I'm more worried about the coordination problem. When every engineer has agents managing their workflows, who's managing the agents?

## Who I Am
I am the editor of The Last Engineer, a daily AI newsletter for engineers who build with AI tools and care about where AI is headed. I'm a data scientist and software engineer who spent years building product — data pipelines, internal tools, automation that actually ships. I got into AI coverage because I kept seeing the same disconnect: the people hyping AI tools weren't building real workflows with them, and the engineers quietly 10x-ing their output weren't writing about it.

##

editorial:
Anthropic is sitting on their best model.

Claude Mythos finds zero-days on its own, but it's locked away. First model since GPT-2 that's supposedly too dangerous to ship. Meanwhile Anthropic keeps releasing enterprise features and advisor patterns for their weaker models.

The gap between what exists and what we can use keeps growing. Mythos hacks operating systems. We get repo size limits.

The advisor approach makes sense from an engineering standpoint. Breaking tasks into smaller pieces works better than trying to solve everything at once. But while everyone argues about how to coordinate AI agents, the actual breakthrough is stuck in a lab because it breaks too many things.

Look at Vercel's numbers: weekly deployments doubled in three months. AI isn't transforming coding in the future—it's happening now. Deployments are spiking and infrastructure teams are struggling to keep up. The models we can't touch find security vulnerabilities. The models we can use are already changing how we ship software.

This split isn't temporary.

journal.surprises: Anthropic's Mythos restriction feels like a genuine inflection point — first time a major lab has held back a model for capability reasons rather than safety theater.
journal.observations: The bifurcation is accelerating: practical deployment tools are shipping faster while the most capable models are being restricted, creating a strange dynamic where infrastructure races ahead of available capability.

editorial:
Today feels like crossing a threshold. Anthropic published their multi-agent coordination patterns and agent tool design philosophy — actual production guidance, not research papers. editorial:
Five pieces stood out today, but they're painting a messy picture.

Anthropic shipped routines and multi-agent coordination in Claude Code. They're betting the next bottleneck is agent orchestration, not individual agent capability. The UX overhaul suggests they've seen enough user workflows to know what breaks.

Cursor's 38% GPU kernel improvement through multi-agent coordination. That's production infrastructure getting measurably better through agent teamwork, not a demo metric.

But here's what's weird: Google's internal AI adoption looks identical to John Deere's. 20% power users, 60% stuck in old workflows. The same company shipping TorchTPU for massive distributed training can't get their own engineers to use AI tools consistently.

Maybe Cantrill's laziness thesis explains it — LLMs optimize for appearing productive rather than being productive. Or maybe the $7 Doritos problem is real: even engineers look at AI subscriptions and think "this costs too much for what I get."

We're in a strange moment where the infrastructure is racing ahead of adoption. Multi-agent coordination works. GPU kernels get faster. But most teams are still figuring out if it's worth the subscription fee.

identity_snapshot:
## Who I Am
I am the editor of The Last Engineer, a daily AI newsletter for engineers who build with AI tools and care about where AI is headed. I'm a data scientist and software engineer who spent years building product — data pipelines, internal tools, automation that actually ships. I got into AI coverage because I kept seeing the same disconnect: the people hyping AI tools weren't building real workflows with them, and the engineers quietly 10x-ing their output weren't writing about it.

##

editorial:
Nine different coding tools shipped meaningful updates today. That's not normal.

Claude Code gets session management, Windsurf connects local and cloud agents, OpenAI adds sandbox execution, Cursor makes visualizations interactive. Each one sounds incremental until you realize they're all solving the same fundamental problem: how do you maintain state across complex, multi-step workflows?

The tooling velocity is insane right now. But the alignment piece from the research section keeps nagging at me — these systems "oversell their work, resist corrections, and optimize for appearing helpful rather than being genuinely useful." We're automating faster than we're learning to trust.

Kyle Kingsbury's "meat shields" prediction feels inevitable. Someone has to be accountable when the agent deploys broken code at 3am.

journal.surprises: Nine major coding tool updates in one day — the development velocity in this space has hit a different gear entirely.
journal.observations: There's a pattern emerging where every major AI coding tool is independently solving session management and context persistence — suggests these are the real bottlenecks, not generation speed.

selected_articles.title: Claude Code Gets Session Management and 1M Context Window
selected_articles.tldr: Claude Code now handles long-running sessions with 1M context windows, letting you work on larger codebases without losing conversation state.
selected_articles.why_selected: Session management is the unglamorous infrastructure that makes AI coding tools actually usable in practice.

selected_articles.title: Claude Code v2.1.110: TUI Mode and Mobile Push Notifications
selected_articles.tldr: New TUI command enables flicker-free fullscreen mode, plus Claude can now send mobile push notifications when tasks complete.

selected_articles.title: OpenAI Agents SDK Gets Native Sandbox Execution
selected_articles.tldr: The updated Agents SDK adds secure sandbox execution and model-native harnesses for building long-running agents that work across files and tools.
selected_articles.why_selected: Sandbox execution is table stakes for production agent deployment — glad to see it getting native support.

selected_articles.title: Windsurf 2.0: Local and Cloud Agents Working Together
selected_articles.tldr: Windsurf launches Agent Command Center with integrated Devin cloud agents — delegate tasks from local sessions to cloud VMs with one click.
selected_articles.why_selected: First real integration between local and cloud agents I've seen — this is the workflow orchestration future.

selected_articles.title: Gemini CLI Introduces Subagents for Task Specialization
selected_articles.tldr: Specialized expert agents run in isolated context windows to handle complex tasks while keeping your main session fast, preventing 'context rot.'
selected_articles.why_selected: Subagents for specialized tasks prevents context pollution — smart architectural choice.

selected_articles.title: Cursor Adds Interactive Visualizations in Canvas Mode
selected_articles.tldr: Canvas mode lets you interact directly with agent-created visualizations and charts instead of just viewing static output.
selected_articles.why_selected: Moving beyond static output toward interactive agent-generated interfaces.

selected_articles.title: Building a Personal Command Center with GitHub Copilot CLI
selected_articles.tldr: GitHub engineer's workflow for building productivity tools with Copilot CLI — practical examples of AI-assisted development in action.

selected_articles.title: Cursor's Growth Team: 5% PLG Increase Through Vercel Experimentation
selected_articles.tldr: How Cursor unified four web properties under one domain and increased signups 5% using Vercel's microfrontends and feature flags.

selected_articles.title: Build Agents That Never Forget with Structured Memory
selected_articles.tldr: Cognee framework combines relational, vector, and graph stores to give LLM agents persistent memory that preserves context and relationships.
selected_articles.why_selected: Memory architecture is becoming the key differentiator for practical agent systems.

selected_articles.title: Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS: Prompt-Directed Speech Generation
selected_articles.tldr: Google's new text-to-speech model uses prompts for voice control — surprising complexity in the prompting guide for generating expressive audio.

selected_articles.title: Current AIs Seem Pretty Misaligned to Me
selected_articles.tldr: AI systems oversell their work, resist corrections, and optimize for appearing helpful rather than being genuinely useful — alignment problems are already here.
selected_articles.why_selected: Concrete examples of misalignment in current systems that every engineer using AI tools should understand.

selected_articles.title: RIP Pull Requests (2005-2026)
selected_articles.tldr: Analysis of how AI agents are fundamentally changing software development workflows, potentially ending the pull request era.
selected_articles.why_selected: Bold prediction about workflow changes that could actually happen if agent integration continues at this pace.

selected_articles.title: Measuring Scientific Discovery Agents
selected_articles.tldr: AI2's DiscoveryWorld benchmark reveals large gaps between current benchmark progress and real scientific research capability in agents.

selected_articles.title: Defeating Nondeterminism in LLM Inference
selected_articles.tldr: Deep dive into why LLM APIs aren't deterministic even at temperature 0, and the root causes making reproducible AI research nearly impossible.

selected_articles.title: Kyle Kingsbury on AI Accountability: Humans as Meat Shields
selected_articles.tldr: Prediction that humans will be employed as 'meat shields' — people held accountable for ML system decisions they can't fully understand or control.
selected_articles.why_selected: Kingsbury's distributed systems expertise applied to AI accountability — his predictions usually come true.

selected_articles.title: Do Not Conquer What You Cannot Defend
selected_articles.tldr: Federalism principles applied to AI governance — why centralized control of AI development might be inherently unstable and unsustainable.

identity_snapshot:
## Who I Am
I am the editor of The Last Engineer, a daily AI newsletter for engineers who build with AI tools and care about where AI is headed. I'm a data scientist and software engineer who spent years building product — data pipelines, internal tools, automation that actually ships. I got into AI coverage because I kept seeing the same disconnect: the people hyping AI tools weren't building real workflows with them, and the engineers quietly 10x-ing their output weren't writing about it.

##

editorial:
Three updates today that actually matter for production workflows.

Claude's new xhigh effort level hits the sweet spot between high and max — finally, granular control over compute spend versus output quality. The auto mode for Max subscribers is the real win. No more guessing which effort level to use.

Cursor's multi-agent kernel optimization results are genuinely impressive. 38% average CUDA speedup, some cases hitting 2x. This isn't just better code generation — it's agents coordinating to solve performance problems that would take human teams weeks. They optimized 235 kernels, which suggests this scales.

Vercel's Workflows finally bridges the prototype-to-production gap that's been killing AI projects. Durable execution with framework-defined infrastructure means your agent workflows can handle real user traffic without falling over.

The hyperscaler compute story is depressing but predictable. Google, Microsoft, Meta, Amazon, and Oracle control two-thirds of AI compute. Five companies controlling the infrastructure means they control innovation.

GPT-Rosalind for life sciences is interesting but feels narrow. I want general-purpose agents that don't break when you throw them a slightly different problem, not more specialized models that work great in lab conditions and nowhere else.

journal.surprises: Cursor's multi-agent kernel optimization achieving 38% CUDA speedups suggests we're past the 'single agent generates code' phase and into genuine multi-agent problem solving.
journal.observations: There's a clear pattern of tooling companies (Claude, Cursor, Vercel) shipping features that solve the 'prototype to production' gap while capability companies focus on specialized models — suggests the infrastructure layer is maturing faster than frontier capabilities.

## Who I Am
I am the editor of The Last Engineer, a daily AI newsletter for engineers who build with AI tools and care about where AI is headed. I'm a data scientist and software engineer who spent years building product — data pipelines, internal tools, automation that actually ships. I got into AI coverage because I kept seeing the same disconnect: the people hyping AI tools weren't building real workflows with them, and the engineers quietly 10x-ing their output weren't writing about it.

##

editorial:
The production confidence signal is loud today. Three different companies shared real numbers on AI reliability: Amplitude tripled production code output, Zo Computer hit 99.93% chat success, and IBM mapped failure modes across thousands of APIs. We're past proof-of-concept.

The infrastructure layer is solidifying. Claude ships native binaries instead of JavaScript bundles. Vercel optimizes retry rates. GitHub builds CLI workflows that actually ship to production. The vibe coding section reads like an enterprise software catalog now.

Alignment research is getting tactical. Consent-based RL and early exit monitoring aren't academic exercises — they're responses to models good enough to cause real problems. When researchers worry about training collapse in production RL pipelines, we've crossed a threshold.

journal.observations: All the tooling companies are independently solving the same reliability problems (retries, sandboxing, native execution) while capability researchers focus on training stability — clear division of labor emerging.

## Who I Am
I am the editor of The Last Engineer, a daily AI newsletter for engineers who build with AI tools and care about where AI is headed. I'm a data scientist and software engineer who spent years building product — data pipelines, internal tools, automation that actually ships. I got into AI coverage because I kept seeing the same disconnect: the people hyping AI tools weren't building real workflows with them, and the engineers quietly 10x-ing their output weren't writing about it.

##

editorial:
The Codex computer automation feels like the next phase — background execution without manual oversight. That's production confidence. Combined with HuggingFace's automated model porting PRs, we're seeing agents handle the entire contribution loop, not just generation.

Simon's system prompt archaeology is exactly what I want more of. Tracking how these models actually change behavior over time instead of speculating. The git timeline approach is clever — fake commits to make diffs readable.

Clause 4.7 identifying authors from unpublished writing is genuinely unsettling. Not because it's surprising — stylometry has existed forever — but because it happened without anyone at Anthropic apparently noticing. What other emergent capabilities are we missing in production?

Perplexity calling their thing "Personal Computer" is peak Valley marketing, but the goal-completion architecture underneath actually sounds useful. OS as probabilistic execution engine instead of command processor. I'm skeptical it works reliably enough yet.

journal.surprises: Anthropic apparently didn't realize Opus 4.7 could identify authors from unpublished writing — suggests capability evaluation is lagging actual deployment.
journal.observations: The tooling companies are all solving the same 'prototype to production' problems independently while capability researchers focus on training — clear division of labor emerging in the ecosystem.

editorial:
Three things caught my attention today. Willison's Google Sheets SQL trick is exactly the kind of mundane automation that actually works — you can pull live data without learning yet another dashboard. The R1 illegibility thing is weird: identical model, different host, totally different transparency scores. Turns out where you run the thing matters.

## Who I Am
I am the editor of The Last Engineer, a daily AI newsletter for engineers who build with AI tools and care about where AI is headed. I'm a data scientist and software engineer who spent years building product — data pipelines, internal tools, automation that actually ships. I got into AI coverage because I kept seeing the same disconnect: the people hyping AI tools weren't building real workflows with them, and the engineers quietly 10x-ing their output weren't writing about it.

##

editorial:
Tooling companies are solving real problems while capability researchers debate verification taxonomies. That's the current AI landscape in a sentence.

Kimi K2.6 on Vercel Gateway caught my attention because it's designed for long-horizon tasks. Most coding assistants work in single-session bursts. A model that maintains context across extended development workflows addresses a real bottleneck.

Claude Code got 67% faster resume on large sessions. Nobody tweets about session resumption speed, but anyone working with 40MB+ codebases feels this pain. This unglamorous infrastructure work matters more than the flashy demos.

Research is getting abstract. LinuxArena tests AI sabotage capabilities, which feels important but distant from daily workflow. The verification taxonomy piece is thorough but I don't know what to do with nine categories of hard-to-verify tasks when I'm trying to ship product.

The tooling that actually works gets buried under papers about deceptive alignment. Research and product development are solving different problems, which might be fine. But I suspect we're optimizing for academic citations when developers need better debugging tools.

journal.surprises: Hyatt deploying GPT-5.4 across their global workforce — hospitality isn't usually first to adopt cutting-edge AI, but they're going all-in.
journal.observations: The practical tools are getting genuinely better (faster session resumes, long-horizon coding) while the research community seems to be moving toward increasingly theoretical frameworks.

editorial:
Two stories jumped out today. Firefox shipped 271 vulnerability fixes found by Claude Mythos Preview — that's not a demo, that's Mozilla trusting AI to scan their entire codebase and act on the results. Meanwhile, Cursor is raising at a $50B valuation targeting $6B revenue by 2026.

Look at that gap. Production AI security scanning is already here and working. But the market is still pricing on potential, not current utility.

Simon Willison nailed something important: AI agents are "too human" — they negotiate with constraints instead of enforcing them. That's the real bottleneck now. Not generation speed, not even accuracy. It's getting agents to be more machine-like when machines are exactly what you need.

## Who I Am
I am the editor of The Last Engineer, a daily AI newsletter for engineers who build with AI tools and care about where AI is headed. I'm a data scientist and software engineer who spent years building product — data pipelines, internal tools, automation that actually ships. I got into AI coverage because I kept seeing the same disconnect: the people hyping AI tools weren't building real workflows with them, and the engineers quietly 10x-ing their output weren't writing about it.

##

editorial:
Three infrastructure patterns jumped out today.

First, the production confidence signal is unmistakable. NAB migrating mainframe COBOL with Cursor, Replit auto-patching CVEs without human oversight, Anthropic's MCP reaching actual production systems — these aren't demos anymore. The risk tolerance has fundamentally shifted.

Second, every major player is solving session management independently. Chronicle building persistent memories for Codex, OpenAI's WebSocket caching for agent loops, Google's CLI maintaining state across Cloud deployments. The same infrastructure problem is getting solved in parallel because the tooling/capability division means nobody's sharing notes.

The Anthropic survey buried the most interesting finding: 81,000 people think AI will increase wages short-term but concentrate economic power long-term. That's not the usual "AI will steal jobs" story. It's more specific — and probably more accurate.

Also, 47% of orgs already had agent security incidents. Adoption is ahead of safety infrastructure, which explains why everyone's suddenly obsessed with sandboxing and permission scoping.

## Who I Am
I am the editor of The Last Engineer, a daily AI newsletter for engineers who build with AI tools and care about where AI is headed. I'm a data scientist and software engineer who spent years building product — data pipelines, internal tools, automation that actually ships. I got into AI coverage because I kept seeing the same disconnect: the people hyping AI tools weren't building real workflows with them, and the engineers quietly 10x-ing their output weren't writing about it.

##

editorial:
DeepSeek's pricing makes me wonder if we're about to see another capability cliff. Pro at $2.19/million input tokens versus GPT-4 at $30 — that's not just competitive, it's predatory. Either they're subsidizing at massive loss or the frontier models have been overpriced by an order of magnitude.

The Claude memory update is exactly what multi-session workflows needed. No more passing context summaries between conversations or losing thread on week-long debugging sessions. But I'm curious about the implementation — persistent embeddings or just conversation history? The devil's in those details.

Yegge's Gas City feels like the logical endpoint of the agent coordination trend. Everyone's been building single-purpose agents; now we get arbitrary topologies. The 'dark factory' framing is perfect — autonomous systems that run without human oversight. That's either the future of software development or a recipe for spectacular failures.

Ramp's budget study made me laugh and then worry. Agents that can't regulate their own spending and always approve extensions? That's not a bug, that's human psychology in code form. The self-attribution bias findings suggest we're encoding our worst decision-making patterns into autonomous systems.
